title: Sample Claude Exam - Form D
description: Generated 60-question variation bank aligned to the Claude architecture foundations study guide.

source.notes: Questions were generated by domain-focused workers, then validated and enriched against the structured study guide.

domains.label: Agentic Architecture & Orchestration
domains.description: Design model-driven agent loops, coordinator-subagent systems, handoffs, hooks, decomposition, and session state.

domains.label: Tool Design & MCP Integration
domains.description: Design reliable tools, MCP integrations, structured errors, scoped tool access, and built-in tool usage.

domains.label: Claude Code Configuration & Workflows
domains.description: Configure Claude Code memory, rules, skills, commands, plan mode, iterative workflows, and CI behavior.

domains.label: Prompt Engineering & Structured Output
domains.description: Improve precision, use few-shot prompting, enforce schema output, validate retries, batch work, and design review architectures.

domains.label: Context Management & Reliability
domains.description: Preserve facts, manage long context, escalate appropriately, propagate errors, explore codebases, calibrate review, and keep provenance.

questions.guideSummary: Tool results must be returned into the conversation so the model can decide whether to continue or finish.
questions.prompt: A subscription-management agent calls `get_entitlements`, the backend receives the entitlements, and then the model asks the user to provide the same entitlement details manually. The application stores the tool output in logs but not in the next model request. What should change?

choices.text: Increase the model temperature so it is more willing to infer entitlement details.
choices.explanation: The problem is missing tool context, not insufficient creativity.

choices.text: Append the `get_entitlements` result as a tool result message before the next model turn.
choices.explanation: Correct. Tool outputs must become part of conversation history for the next reasoning step.

choices.text: Send the tool output only to the user and ask the model to continue from the user's reply.
choices.explanation: This creates an unnecessary handoff and still leaves the model without the direct tool result until the user responds.

choices.text: Treat any successful entitlement lookup as final and end the loop immediately.
choices.explanation: The model may still need to reason over the entitlements and take another action.

guideReferences: Domain 2, Task 2.1: Design effective tool interfaces with clear descriptions and boundaries
questions.guideSummary: When tools overlap semantically, names and descriptions should make boundaries explicit rather than relying on Claude to infer intent.
questions.prompt: A healthcare scheduling assistant has `find_visit`, `search_visit`, and `get_visit`. One returns upcoming appointments by patient ID, one searches historical visits by diagnosis code, and one retrieves a single visit by visit ID. Because their descriptions all mention "visit lookup," Claude frequently calls the wrong one. What is the best redesign?

choices.text: Rename and describe them as `list_upcoming_appointments_by_patient`, `search_past_visits_by_diagnosis`, and `get_visit_by_id`, including required identifiers and outputs.
choices.explanation: Correct. Differentiated names, inputs, outputs, and boundaries give Claude concrete selection signals.

choices.text: Keep the names and add a hidden server-side router that guesses which visit operation Claude intended.
choices.explanation: A hidden router adds another guessing layer instead of making the tool interface clear.

choices.text: Delete the historical search tool because upcoming appointments are more common.
choices.explanation: Removing a needed tool does not solve the interface design problem.

choices.text: Tell Claude to ask the user which tool to use whenever a visit request appears.
choices.explanation: Users should not need to know internal tool names for routine routing.

questions.guideSummary: Covers using Claude Code memory hierarchy to apply repo-wide and directory-specific instructions appropriately.
questions.prompt: A monorepo has shared TypeScript standards plus a payments service with stricter PCI handling rules. Engineers want Claude Code to load the TypeScript standards everywhere, but load the PCI guidance only when working under services/payments. What configuration best fits the hierarchy?

choices.text: Put all instructions in the payments directory so the strictest guidance is the source of truth.
choices.explanation: That would fail to load shared TypeScript standards for other parts of the repo and over-scopes payment-specific guidance.

choices.text: Put PCI guidance in every engineer's user-level CLAUDE.md to avoid committing sensitive rules.
choices.explanation: Shared service rules should be controlled consistently. User memory creates drift and uneven behavior.

choices.text: Put shared TypeScript standards in project CLAUDE.md and payments-specific PCI guidance in a scoped memory or rule for services/payments.
choices.explanation: Correct. Shared standards belong at project scope, while service-specific requirements should be scoped to the relevant path.

choices.text: Create one slash command for TypeScript edits and another for payments edits.
choices.explanation: Always-relevant coding standards are better represented as memory or rules than manual commands.

guideReferences: Domain 4, Task 4.1: Design prompts with explicit criteria to improve precision and reduce false positives
questions.guideSummary: Covers explicit review criteria and severity definitions to reduce noisy false positives.
questions.prompt: An accessibility review prompt says, 'Flag anything that may reduce accessibility.' The bot reports every missing `aria-label`, including buttons that already have visible text, while missing keyboard traps in modal dialogs. What prompt revision best improves review quality?

choices.text: Ask Claude to report only accessibility issues that appear in at least three files.
choices.explanation: Frequency is not severity. One keyboard trap can be a serious issue.

choices.text: Define reportable accessibility failures and skip cases, with severity examples such as keyboard traps versus redundant labels.
choices.explanation: Correct. Explicit report and skip criteria focus the model on real accessibility defects instead of noisy patterns.

choices.text: Tell Claude to avoid comments about ARIA because those created false positives.
choices.explanation: That may suppress legitimate ARIA issues instead of distinguishing redundant from necessary labels.

choices.text: Increase the maximum response length so Claude can explain each accessibility comment in more detail.
choices.explanation: Longer explanations do not fix poor issue selection.

skills: Maintain structured case facts.
Persist multi-issue state separately from summaries.
Trim tool results to relevant fields.
Place summaries early and use explicit section headers.
knowledge: Progressive summaries can blur amounts, dates, and commitments.
Lost-in-the-middle effects hide middle input details.
Verbose tool results can consume context without adding value.
Complete conversation history helps coherence.
guideReferences: Domain 5, Task 5.1: Manage conversation context to preserve critical information across long interactions
questions.guideSummary: Store critical long-context facts such as dates, passenger names, fees, and commitments in structured state.
questions.prompt: A travel assistant helps a family rebook three flights, apply two credits, and coordinate hotel changes. After several tool calls, it confuses the outbound and return dates and applies the wrong credit to the wrong passenger. What is the best reliability improvement?

choices.text: Ask the model to double-check dates before every response.
choices.explanation: Double-checking helps only if the exact facts remain accessible and unambiguous.

choices.text: Summarize the conversation after every tool call in natural language.
choices.explanation: Frequent prose summaries can still blur dates, amounts, and passenger-specific commitments.

choices.text: Keep a structured trip state with passengers, dates, ticket numbers, credit amounts, restrictions, and unresolved actions, updating it after each tool result.
choices.explanation: Correct. Long rebooking workflows need durable structured facts rather than relying on prose summaries.

choices.text: Send all airline and hotel tool responses unchanged into the next turn.
choices.explanation: Verbose tool output can overwhelm context and bury the relevant fields.

guideReferences: Domain 1, Task 1.1: Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution
questions.guideSummary: The model's end_turn signal should terminate the loop; tool_use should continue it, independent of response wording.
questions.prompt: A quote-generation loop keeps asking Claude for another step after the model returns a final quote with stop_reason `end_turn`, because the final text includes the phrase "next steps." This causes duplicate quotes. What is the correct loop behavior?

choices.text: Continue the loop whenever the final answer mentions possible next steps.
choices.explanation: This is the brittle natural-language heuristic causing the duplicate work.

choices.text: Require the model to avoid the words "next steps" in final answers.
choices.explanation: Changing wording is fragile and does not fix loop control.

choices.text: End the loop after a fixed two model calls for all quote requests.
choices.explanation: A fixed count will be too short for some quotes and too long for others.

choices.text: Terminate the loop when stop_reason is `end_turn`, regardless of incidental words in the final answer.
choices.explanation: Correct. Formal stop reasons, not text heuristics, should govern loop completion.

questions.guideSummary: Tool descriptions should not contain broad usage claims that bias selection beyond the tool's real boundary.
questions.prompt: An analytics assistant has a `get_dashboard_snapshot` tool whose description starts, "Always use this for metrics questions." The tool only returns cached executive dashboards, but Claude uses it for ad hoc metric definitions that require querying the metrics catalog. What is the best fix?

choices.text: Make the dashboard snapshot tool call the catalog tool internally for every request.
choices.explanation: That obscures boundaries and may add unnecessary work for genuine dashboard requests.

choices.text: Move the phrase "Always use this" into the system prompt so it is more visible.
choices.explanation: That strengthens the incorrect selection bias instead of fixing it.

choices.text: Remove examples from all tool descriptions so no tool appears preferred.
choices.explanation: Examples are useful when accurate; the problem is an inaccurate boundary claim.

choices.text: Revise the description to say it returns cached dashboard snapshots only, list when to use it, and state that metric-definition questions require the catalog tool.
choices.explanation: Correct. The broad "always use" wording biases selection and should be replaced with accurate boundaries.

questions.guideSummary: Covers using @import to keep shared standards modular instead of duplicating instructions.
questions.prompt: Three packages each include nearly identical Claude Code instructions for error handling. The team keeps forgetting to update all copies when the standard changes. They want one maintained source while still loading the guidance for relevant packages. What is the best approach?

choices.text: Keep the duplicates because local copies make each package self-contained.
choices.explanation: The drift problem shows that duplicate instructions are already failing operationally.

choices.text: Create a focused shared standards file and reference it with @import from the relevant CLAUDE.md or rule files.
choices.explanation: Correct. Imports keep memory modular and avoid duplicate instruction drift.

choices.text: Move the error handling standard into a personal skill for the package maintainers.
choices.explanation: A shared coding standard should not depend on personal skills.

choices.text: Replace the detailed standard with a short instruction to follow existing package style.
choices.explanation: That loses explicit guidance and does not solve cases where existing style is inconsistent.

questions.guideSummary: Covers disabling noisy categories while preserving high-signal review checks.
questions.prompt: A configuration review assistant flags every environment variable that lacks a comment. The team's incidents are actually caused by missing required variables, secrets accidentally committed, and unsafe defaults. What is the best adjustment?

choices.text: Remove the comment-style category and define reportable configuration risks with concrete examples of missing required values, committed secrets, and unsafe defaults.
choices.explanation: Correct. This eliminates a noisy category while sharpening the real review criteria.

choices.text: Keep all categories but ask Claude to rank comment issues below security issues.
choices.explanation: Ranking still leaves noisy findings in the report and weakens developer trust.

choices.text: Require Claude to include a remediation command for every comment-related finding.
choices.explanation: Making non-issues easier to fix does not make them worth reporting.

choices.text: Ask Claude to infer which environment variables are important from their names.
choices.explanation: Name-based inference is less reliable than explicit criteria for the actual failure modes.

skills: Add escalation criteria with examples.
Honor explicit human-agent requests immediately.
Acknowledge frustration while resolving straightforward cases.
Ask for identifiers rather than guessing among multiple matches.
knowledge: Escalation triggers include human requests, policy gaps, and inability to progress.
Straightforward issues may be resolved even when a customer is frustrated unless they request a human.
Sentiment and self-reported confidence are weak proxies for complexity.
Multiple matches require clarification.
guideReferences: Domain 5, Task 5.2: Design effective escalation and ambiguity resolution patterns
questions.guideSummary: Frustration alone should not trigger escalation when the agent can resolve a straightforward case.
questions.prompt: A customer writes, 'This is ridiculous, I just need my shipping address fixed.' The order is unshipped, the new address passes validation, and the customer has not asked for a human. What should the support agent do?

choices.text: Escalate because the customer's frustration indicates the issue is too sensitive for automation.
choices.explanation: Sentiment alone is a weak proxy for complexity; the case is straightforward and actionable.

choices.text: Ask whether the customer wants a human before taking any action.
choices.explanation: This adds friction when the user asked for a concrete fix and did not request escalation.

choices.text: Refuse to update the address until the customer restates the request more calmly.
choices.explanation: The agent should not punish frustration when the request is clear and within policy.

choices.text: Acknowledge the frustration, confirm the validated address change, and complete the update if authorized.
choices.explanation: Correct. The agent can respect the tone while resolving a straightforward issue.

guideReferences: Domain 1, Task 1.2: Orchestrate multi-agent systems with coordinator-subagent patterns
questions.guideSummary: Coordinator-subagent orchestration should partition scope to reduce duplicate work and missing coverage.
questions.prompt: A legal discovery system sends every document to privilege, relevance, and timeline subagents. Reviewers see repeated summaries and still miss documents that need timeline-only follow-up. What should the coordinator do?

choices.text: Require each subagent to include a section explaining what the other subagents should do next.
choices.explanation: That pushes coordination into specialists and can create conflicting routing advice.

choices.text: Deduplicate repeated summaries after all subagents have processed every document.
choices.explanation: Deduplication reduces report noise but does not prevent wasted work or missed targeted follow-up.

choices.text: Partition documents and questions by review need, delegate scoped tasks to specialists, and aggregate gaps for targeted follow-up.
choices.explanation: Correct. The coordinator should manage scope, delegation, aggregation, and gap handling.

choices.text: Use only the relevance subagent and ask it to flag privilege and timeline issues when obvious.
choices.explanation: A single specialist doing opportunistic checks loses the coverage benefits of specialists.

skills: Return errorCategory, isRetryable, and readable descriptions.
Mark business-rule errors as non-retryable with customer-safe explanations.
Recover locally from transient failures where possible.

Transient, validation, business, and permission error categories.

guideReferences: Domain 2, Task 2.2: Implement structured error responses for MCP tools
questions.guideSummary: Validation errors should be structured, non-retryable until corrected, and specific enough for the agent to ask for the right missing or malformed input.
questions.prompt: A `tokenize_payment_method` MCP tool fails when the expiration date is malformed. It currently returns "tokenization failed," and Claude retries the same call three times. What should the error response include?

choices.text: `isError: false` with `token: null`, because the tool completed its validation step successfully.
choices.explanation: The requested tokenization did not succeed; a null success response hides the failure semantics.

choices.text: `isError: true` with `isRetryable: true`, because the user might later provide a valid date.
choices.explanation: Retrying the same call will not help; the current error is non-retryable until input changes.

choices.text: `isError: true`, `errorCategory: validation`, `isRetryable: false`, the invalid field name, and a safe explanation of the expected expiration-date format.
choices.explanation: Correct. The agent can only recover by getting corrected input, so the response should identify the validation problem.

choices.text: Only the raw gateway code, because exposing validation details may confuse Claude.
choices.explanation: A raw code alone gives Claude less recovery context than a normalized validation response.

questions.guideSummary: Covers choosing a skill over always-loaded memory for a verbose on-demand review workflow.
questions.prompt: The infrastructure team has a long Terraform review checklist that is only needed during infrastructure reviews. When placed in CLAUDE.md, Claude Code starts mentioning Terraform concerns during ordinary application edits. What should the team use instead?

choices.text: Move the Terraform checklist to user-level CLAUDE.md for all infrastructure engineers.
choices.explanation: That makes a team workflow personal and still risks drift across engineers.

choices.text: Leave it in CLAUDE.md because extra caution is harmless.
choices.explanation: Irrelevant instructions can distract Claude Code and reduce task focus.

choices.text: Delete the checklist and rely on Claude Code's general Terraform knowledge.
choices.explanation: The checklist is useful; it just needs to be invoked at the right time.

choices.text: Create an on-demand skill or project command for the Terraform review workflow rather than always loading the checklist.
choices.explanation: Correct. Verbose workflows that are only sometimes needed should be invoked on demand, not loaded into every task.

guideReferences: Domain 4, Task 4.2: Apply few-shot prompting to improve output consistency and quality
questions.guideSummary: Covers few-shot examples for format consistency and ambiguous extraction cases.
questions.prompt: A changelog extractor must classify entries as `breaking`, `feature`, `fix`, or `docs`. It often marks any sentence with 'removed old code' as breaking, even when the entry describes internal cleanup, and misses breaking changes phrased as 'clients must now send'. What is the best prompt improvement?

choices.text: Make `breaking` the default class whenever the text contains the words 'removed' or 'must'.
choices.explanation: Keyword defaults create the same false positives and miss context.

choices.text: Remove the `breaking` class and ask humans to identify those entries manually.
choices.explanation: That avoids the hardest category instead of improving model judgment.

choices.text: Ask Claude to write a longer rationale for each changelog classification.
choices.explanation: Rationales may help auditing, but examples are the direct way to teach the boundary.

choices.text: Add few-shot examples that contrast internal removals with user-facing breaking changes and show the desired output format.
choices.explanation: Correct. Targeted examples teach the ambiguous distinction and the structured output pattern.

skills: Return failure type, attempted query, partial results, and alternatives.
Distinguish empty successful results from failures.
Recover locally before escalating.
Annotate synthesis coverage gaps.
knowledge: Structured error context enables recovery decisions.
Access failures differ from valid empty results.
Generic errors hide useful recovery context.
Suppressing errors and terminating on single failures are both anti-patterns.
guideReferences: Domain 5, Task 5.3: Implement error propagation strategies across multi-agent systems
questions.guideSummary: Avoid both suppressing subagent errors and aborting useful synthesis when partial results can be annotated.
questions.prompt: A vendor-risk coordinator receives complete financial and security findings, but the privacy subagent fails because a required document link returns 404. The procurement team needs a same-day preliminary decision. What should the coordinator do?

choices.text: Mark privacy risk as low because no privacy issue was found.
choices.explanation: The privacy review failed; no issue found is not the same as no issue exists.

choices.text: Cancel the entire review because one subagent failed.
choices.explanation: Terminating on a single failure discards useful findings and may be unnecessary for a preliminary decision.

choices.text: Ask the financial subagent to infer privacy risk from vendor size and revenue.
choices.explanation: Inferring a missing review from unrelated evidence creates unsupported conclusions.

choices.text: Produce the preliminary decision using available findings and clearly annotate the missing privacy-document review with failure details and next steps.
choices.explanation: Correct. Structured partial synthesis with coverage gaps supports action without hiding the error.

questions.guideSummary: The coordinator is the recovery hub when subagent work fails, returns incomplete coverage, or needs reassignment.
questions.prompt: A fraud investigation coordinator delegates transaction clustering to one subagent and device analysis to another. The device subagent times out. The current system returns a final report without mentioning the missing device analysis. What should the coordinator do?

choices.text: Let the transaction subagent infer device risk from transaction patterns.
choices.explanation: That asks one specialist to cover a different missing scope without the required context.

choices.text: Hide failed subagent work from the final report to avoid confusing the user.
choices.explanation: Suppressing a known gap makes the synthesis misleading.

choices.text: Have the failed subagent send its partial state directly to the user.
choices.explanation: The coordinator, not the subagent, should own final aggregation and user communication.

choices.text: Track the failed delegation, retry or reassign the device analysis if appropriate, and clearly represent any remaining gap in the final synthesis.
choices.explanation: Correct. Coordinator responsibilities include recovery, gap handling, and aggregation.

Mark business-rule errors as non-retryable with customer-safe explanations.

knowledge: MCP isError flag pattern.
Transient, validation, business, and permission error categories.

guideReferences: Domain 2, Task 2.2: Implement structured error responses for MCP tools
questions.guideSummary: Permission failures should be represented as errors, while valid no-result searches should remain successful empty results.
questions.prompt: A `search_contracts` MCP tool searches legal contracts. If no contracts match, it returns an empty result. If the user lacks access to the legal workspace, it also returns an empty result. Claude then says no contracts exist. What response pattern is best?

choices.text: Return success with an empty list only for valid no-match searches; return `isError: true` with a permission category and safe explanation for access denial.
choices.explanation: Correct. The two outcomes need different semantics so Claude can respond accurately.

choices.text: Continue returning empty results, but include a hidden server log for access-denied cases.
choices.explanation: The agent still cannot distinguish no data from no permission.

choices.text: Return all matching contract titles but redact the body when access is denied.
choices.explanation: That may leak information and still does not model the permission failure cleanly.

choices.text: Ask Claude to infer permission errors when a query has no results.
choices.explanation: Inference from absence is unreliable and exactly what structured error metadata avoids.

questions.guideSummary: Covers adding skill argument hints and allowed tools for reliable invocation.
questions.prompt: A team skill generates incident retrospectives from a ticket ID. New users often forget to include the ticket ID, and the skill should not be able to edit repository files. What frontmatter choices would most improve the skill?

choices.text: Have the skill infer the ticket ID from the current git branch name instead of asking the user for it.
choices.explanation: Branch names do not reliably contain the ticket ID and may belong to a different ticket, so the retrospective can target the wrong incident. An argument-hint makes the required input explicit at invocation.

choices.text: Add an argument-hint for the ticket ID and restrict allowed-tools to the read-only tools needed by the retrospective workflow.
choices.explanation: Correct. Argument hints clarify required inputs, and allowed-tools limits what the skill can do.

choices.text: Give the skill all tools so it can recover if users provide incomplete inputs.
choices.explanation: Broader tool access does not solve missing arguments and increases unnecessary capability.

choices.text: Convert the skill into a path-specific rule for incident markdown files.
choices.explanation: The workflow is invoked by ticket ID and tool policy, not merely by editing a path.

questions.guideSummary: Covers examples that demonstrate extraction across varied document formats and reduce hallucination.
questions.prompt: A grant application extractor must output funding amount, matching-funds requirement, and reporting deadline. It performs well on forms but poorly on narrative proposals where deadlines are embedded in paragraphs. It also invents matching-funds requirements for programs that do not mention them. What is the best next step?

choices.text: Make matching funds a required non-null field because downstream scoring needs it.
choices.explanation: Required non-null fields encourage fabrication when the document omits the value.

choices.text: Add few-shot examples covering forms and narrative proposals, including examples where missing matching-funds information returns null.
choices.explanation: Correct. The examples teach varied source layouts and the null-rather-than-invent behavior.

choices.text: Reject narrative proposals and process only structured forms.
choices.explanation: That avoids a supported input type instead of improving extraction behavior.

choices.text: Use a post-processing rule that sets matching funds to 0 whenever the model returns null.
choices.explanation: Null may mean absent from the source, not zero. Replacing it loses important uncertainty.

guideReferences: Domain 5, Task 5.4: Manage context effectively in large codebase exploration
questions.guideSummary: Use recovery manifests and scratchpads when resuming long codebase investigations.
questions.prompt: A repository exploration session crashes after mapping routing, persistence, and background jobs. On resume, the assistant has a short conversation summary but no list of files read or open hypotheses. What practice should have been in place?

choices.text: A larger context window so the original transcript could be retained.
choices.explanation: More context can help, but crash recovery should not depend on a single live transcript.

choices.text: A recovery manifest containing files inspected, key findings, unresolved questions, and next planned searches.
choices.explanation: Correct. Structured state exports support reliable crash recovery and prevent repeating or forgetting exploration.

choices.text: A rule that exploration sessions must finish in one uninterrupted run.
choices.explanation: Large codebase work commonly spans sessions; reliability comes from resumable state.

questions.guideSummary: Independent subagents run concurrently only when the coordinator emits multiple Task calls in a single response; one Task call per turn serializes the work.
questions.prompt: A due-diligence coordinator must run three independent specialist reviews -- financials, litigation history, and IP portfolio -- that do not depend on each other. The coordinator has `Task` and correctly delegates all three, but each turn it issues one `Task` call, waits for that subagent to finish, then issues the next. End-to-end latency is roughly the sum of the three reviews. The team wants them to overlap. What should the coordinator do?

choices.text: Keep the one-call-per-turn pattern but raise each subagent's allowed token budget so individual reviews finish faster.
choices.explanation: A larger token budget does not make serialized turns overlap; the calls still run one after another.

choices.text: Have the financials subagent invoke the litigation subagent, which then invokes the IP subagent, chaining them without returning to the coordinator.
choices.explanation: Sibling-to-sibling chaining is still sequential and removes coordinator aggregation and recovery.

choices.text: Emit all three `Task` calls in a single response so the independent subagents run concurrently before the coordinator aggregates them.
choices.explanation: Correct. Multiple Task calls in one response are dispatched together; issuing one call per turn forces them to run sequentially.

choices.text: Merge the three reviews into one subagent prompt so a single Task call covers all three areas at once.
choices.explanation: Collapsing into one subagent removes specialization and still processes the three areas within a single serial invocation.

The hotel agent accidentally charges a card while trying to estimate a deposit. What tool distribution should you implement?

choices.text: Keep all tools available and rely on stronger warnings in each subagent prompt.
choices.explanation: Warnings do not provide the reliability of removing irrelevant high-impact tools.

choices.text: Give each subagent only its role-specific tools, and expose narrow read-only customer and itinerary context where cross-role information is needed.
choices.explanation: Correct. Role-appropriate tool sets reduce harmful misuse while still allowing limited shared context.

choices.text: Move payment tools to the hotel agent only, since hotels most often discuss deposits.
choices.explanation: That still grants payment authority to a non-payment role and creates other gaps.

choices.text: Force every subagent call to use `tool_choice: any` so the model cannot answer without a tool.
choices.explanation: `any` does not choose the right tool or enforce role boundaries.

choices.text: A root CLAUDE.md section titled Mobile Rules with instructions to ignore it for web work.

choices.text: A directory CLAUDE.md only under apps/ios because it has the most screens.
choices.explanation: That misses matching mobile screen files in other directories.

choices.text: Keep the fields required so every downstream record is complete.
choices.explanation: Required fields force the model toward guesses when source data is absent.

choices.text: Use free-text output so Claude can explain uncertainty instead of fitting a schema.
choices.explanation: Free text gives up structured downstream integration. The schema should represent uncertainty with nulls.

choices.text: Make fields nullable where the source may omit them and instruct Claude to return null unless the value is directly stated.
choices.explanation: Correct. Nullable fields plus explicit no-fabrication instructions reduce invented values.

choices.text: Default missing allergies to 'none' and missing language to 'English' in post-processing.
choices.explanation: Defaults can create false clinical records when the source is silent.

choices.text: Bypass review for all high-confidence fields because the overall accuracy is high.
choices.explanation: Aggregate accuracy can hide a weak field or document segment.

choices.text: Bypass review only for typed fields and permanently send all handwritten fields to humans.
choices.explanation: That may be conservative, but calibration is needed to set justified thresholds rather than permanent blanket rules.

choices.text: Ask the model to output lower confidence for any field that appears handwritten.
choices.explanation: Confidence labels need empirical calibration; prompting lower confidence does not measure accuracy.

choices.text: Sample high-confidence outputs by form type and field, especially handwritten dosage fields, and calibrate routing thresholds with labeled review data.
choices.explanation: Correct. Stratified high-confidence sampling reveals whether confidence is reliable where risk is highest.

choices.text: Force `classify_case` for the first model turn, then switch back to `auto` for role-appropriate follow-up tools.
choices.explanation: Correct. The required first call is enforced without blocking later tool choice.

questions.guideSummary: Covers choosing direct execution and matching verification scope to an explicitly bounded request without unrequested scope creep.
questions.prompt: After a package rename, an engineer asks Claude Code to update a single import path in one service file. They give the exact old and new module paths and ask Claude Code to run that service's typecheck to confirm. The renamed module is widely consumed elsewhere in the repo, but the engineer says a separate codemod already handled the other call sites and they only want this one straggler fixed and verified now. What is the best workflow?

choices.text: Make the edit, then run a repo-wide typecheck and grep for the old module path everywhere to confirm no other call site is stale.
choices.explanation: This is a reasonable instinct, but the engineer already stated a codemod handled the other call sites and asked only for the service typecheck. Expanding verification beyond the stated scope adds time without being requested.

choices.text: Use direct execution to make the one import edit, then run the requested service typecheck to confirm it resolves.
choices.explanation: Correct. The edit is exact and the engineer has explicitly bounded both the scope and the verification, so direct execution with the requested typecheck is appropriate and sufficient.

choices.text: Start plan mode to review the rename's blast radius before touching the file.
choices.explanation: Plan mode fits broad or uncertain work. Here the file, the exact replacement, and the verification are all known and narrow, so planning adds overhead without reducing risk.

choices.text: Defer the edit and ask the engineer to confirm the codemod really covered every other call site first.
choices.explanation: The straggler fix is independent and safe to apply now, and the requested typecheck will catch a local break. Blocking on a question about other files stalls a change the engineer already scoped.

choices.explanation: Reading and reasoning predicts an outcome but never observes the real run, so it can declare a pass that the suite would actually fail.

choices.text: Use Bash to run `pnpm --filter @work-kit/invoices test` and read the captured stdout and exit status to see pass or fail and any failure detail.
choices.explanation: Correct. Establishing pass or fail requires executing the command, and Bash runs the package script and returns the real output and exit status the agent needs to act on.

choices.text: Use Edit to adjust the failing assertions to match the new behavior first, then assume the suite passes.
choices.explanation: Editing assertions before running the suite assumes the outcome and can mask a real regression; the suite must be executed before changing any test.
